chunk_summary: This section discusses ferritin interpretation in the context of inflammation, focusing on cost-effective test selection in clinical settings for micronutrient nutrition management.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ferritin as a biomarker for iron status in the presence of inflammation.
Learn cost-effective strategies for selecting micronutrient tests in clinical practice.
Identify appropriate ferritin cutoff values for diagnosing iron deficiency in inflammatory conditions.
Explore guidelines for managing nutritional anemia in children.
key_facts: Ferritin levels can be elevated in inflammatory states, making interpretation challenging.
WHO guidelines suggest ferritin cutoffs of <30 µg/L in the presence of inflammation to diagnose iron deficiency.
The IAP recommends using ferritin alongside C-reactive protein (CRP) or alpha-1-acid glycoprotein (AGP) to account for inflammation.
Daily iron supplementation is recommended for children with confirmed iron deficiency anemia, as per consensus guidelines.
Cost-effective test selection involves prioritizing ferritin and CRP/AGP over more expensive panels in resource-limited settings.
reference_quote: "Ferritin concentrations <30 µg/L in the presence of inflammation are indicative of iron deficiency." (Source: WHO guidelines)
document_key_topics: Ferritin as a biomarker in inflammation
